title: Parsing Ambiguity
concept: Sentences can have multiple valid parse trees ('I saw the man with a telescope' - who has the telescope?), which parsing models must resolve.

exercises.prompt: Before we look at ambiguity, what is the main goal of a sentence parser in NLP?

options.text: Analyze the grammatical structure of a sentence

exercises.explain: A parser's job is to analyze the grammatical structure of a sentence to understand how the words relate to one another.

cards.title: Double Meanings
cards.body: Have you ever said something that was taken the completely wrong way? Human language is full of double meanings. For computers trying to read, these grammatical double-takes are a major puzzle!

cards.title: What is Parsing Ambiguity?
cards.body: Parsing ambiguity occurs when a single sentence can be broken down into more than one valid grammatical structure (parse tree). Because words can play different roles or connect to different parts of a sentence, computers get confused about who is doing what.

cards.title: The Fork in the Road
cards.body: Think of a sentence like a road sign that says 'No parking signs'. Does it mean 'It is forbidden to park signs here,' or 'Signs that say "No Parking" are not allowed'? Both readings are grammatically possible, but they lead down two completely different mental roads. A parser has to choose the correct path!

cards.title: The Telescope Trouble
cards.body:
Take the classic sentence: 'I saw the man with a telescope.' 

• Tree A: I used a telescope to see the man.
• Tree B: I saw a man, and that man was holding a telescope.

Both trees are 100% grammatically correct, leaving the computer with a double meaning to solve!

cards.title: Grammar Isn't Enough
cards.body: It's easy to think that if a computer knows all the rules of grammar, it can parse any sentence perfectly. But grammar rules only tell us what is possible, not what is actually meant. A sentence can be perfectly grammatical but still mean two different things!

cards.title: One Sentence, Two Trees
cards.body: See how the same phrase changes meaning depending on where we attach 'with a telescope' in the grammatical tree structure.

right.text: The telescope is attached to 'the man' (The man is the one holding the telescope).

left.text: The telescope is attached to the action 'saw' (I used the telescope to look).

exercises.prompt: If you parse the sentence 'I ate the chicken with a fork,' why might a computer struggle with parsing ambiguity?

options.text: Because it does not know what a chicken is.
It can't tell if you used a fork to eat, or if the chicken was holding a fork.
Because the word 'chicken' can only be a verb.
Because the sentence is grammatically incorrect.

exercises.explain: Because the sentence has two valid structural interpretations (did the chicken have the fork, or did you use the fork?), it creates two different parse trees.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a sentence is grammatically correct, it can only have one unique parse tree.
exercises.explain: Ambiguity is a natural part of human language. A sentence can be perfectly correct and still have multiple valid parse trees.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe this parsing challenge.
exercises.sentence: When a sentence has more than one valid parse tree, it is called parsing [blank].
exercises.explain: Parsing ambiguity occurs when a sentence can be diagrammed in multiple valid ways, leaving the AI to decide which structure was actually intended.

exercises.prompt: Which of these sentences contains a structural ambiguity that would result in multiple parse trees?

options.text: She saw the dog with the binoculars.

exercises.explain: Option B is correct because the phrase 'with the telescope' can attach to 'saw' (how I looked) or 'man' (what the man had). Option A is not ambiguous, and Option C is just a simple statement.

exercises.prompt: Since human language has so many double meanings, how do modern AI parsing models decide which parse tree is the correct one?

options.text: By asking the user every time

exercises.explain: To resolve ambiguity, computers look at the surrounding context and use probabilities calculated from millions of human-written sentences. This helps them figure out the most likely meaning!
